items.evidence_text:
Client 2: Um, net we take home is like… four thousand a month?
Advisor: Got it—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross, before taxes. Do you know the gross annual number, roughly?
Client 1: Yeah, gross is about fifty thousand a year—actually $50,450. It’s basically all rental income.
Client 2: Wait, I thought it was closer to fifty-five.
Client 1: No—fifty-five was when the unit was fully occupied. Last year annualized was $50,450.

Advisor: Monthly expenses next. What do you two spend in a normal month—everything living-related, not counting any debt payments?
Client 2: We’re pretty lean… maybe like two grand?
Client 1: It’s a bit higher. About $2,200 a month.
Client 2: Yeah—sorry—$2,200 sounds right when you add the groceries and the utilities. I always forget one of them.
Advisor: No problem. I’m writing down $2,200 per month for total household expenses.

Client 2: Um, net we take home is like… four thousand a month?
Advisor: Got it—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross, before taxes. Do you know the gross annual number, roughly?
Client 1: Yeah, gross is about fifty thousand a year—actually $50,450. It’s basically all rental income.
Client 2: Wait, I thought it was closer to fifty-five.
Client 1: No—fifty-five was when the unit was fully occupied. Last year annualized was $50,450.

Client 1: Correct.
Client 2: Yes.
Advisor: And investable assets—excluding the home—are roughly $176,850 total across the $99,400 taxable, $42,850 retirement, $22,100 joint account, and $12,500 private markets. That match what you expect?
Client 1: Yes, that’s right.
Client 2: Yeah, that sounds right.

Advisor: I hear you, Mary. We’ll talk about what it is and how liquid it is in a bit.
Advisor: Last asset category: your home. You mentioned it’s paid off—what’s the approximate value you use for the primary residence?
Client 1: About $149,400.
Client 2: That seems low but… yeah, that’s what the last estimate said.
Advisor: Okay, we’ll stick with about $149,400 for now and can update later if you get a new estimate.

Advisor: Hi William, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Thanks for making the time today.
Client 1: Hi Alex. Yeah, thanks for seeing us.
Client 2: Hi. Sorry, we were a couple minutes—traffic.
Advisor: No worries at all. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this meeting feel useful for you two?
Client 1: Cash flow first. With the support stuff from my prior marriage, I just want to be sure we’re not kidding ourselves.

Client 2: Hi. Sorry, we were a couple minutes—traffic.
Advisor: No worries at all. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this meeting feel useful for you two?
Client 1: Cash flow first. With the support stuff from my prior marriage, I just want to be sure we’re not kidding ourselves.
Client 2: And I want to know we’re not, like, one surprise away from panic.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll start with the monthly picture, then assets, then any debts and insurance gaps. Sound okay, Mary?

Client 2: Um, net we take home is like… four thousand a month?
Advisor: Got it—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross, before taxes. Do you know the gross annual number, roughly?
Client 1: Yeah, gross is about fifty thousand a year—actually $50,450. It’s basically all rental income.
Client 2: Wait, I thought it was closer to fifty-five.
Client 1: No—fifty-five was when the unit was fully occupied. Last year annualized was $50,450.

Client 2: California.
Client 1: Yep, CA.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, taxes—when we talked earlier about cash flow and the rental income, I want to make sure I’m using the right bracket. Do you think of yourselves as around the 35% federal bracket right now?
Client 2: Um, I thought it was like thirty—
Client 1: It’s 35%, Mary. We’re in that 35% bracket.

Advisor: Alright—before we jump in, how's your week been?
Client 2: Busy, but good. Okay—yeah.
Advisor: Busy but good—I hear you, Mary. Okay, going back to what you said earlier about wanting a buffer because the rental income feels steady month to month… that conservative posture you both described—would you say your overall risk tolerance is conservative?
Client 1: Yeah, conservative. I’m not looking to swing for the fences.
Client 2: Conservative, definitely. I get nervous when it drops.

Advisor: Got it. And just so I don’t mis-state your household situation—William, you’ve both mentioned prior marriage obligations before. Are you two currently remarried, like a second marriage for at least one of you?
Client 2: Yes, we’re remarried—second marriage.
Client 1: Right—secondly wedded. That’s the phrase, I guess.
Advisor: Thank you. And I’m going to ask one slightly “industry” question—when we talk about where you’re getting advice, you’re working with me as a U.S. registered investment advisor—so, U.S. RIA. Does that sound right, or am I mixing that up with a brokerage relationship?
Client 2: Um, I thought you were like—wait, is RIA the same as a broker?

Client 2: Hi. Sorry, we were a couple minutes—traffic.
Advisor: No worries at all. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this meeting feel useful for you two?
Client 1: Cash flow first. With the support stuff from my prior marriage, I just want to be sure we’re not kidding ourselves.
Client 2: And I want to know we’re not, like, one surprise away from panic.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll start with the monthly picture, then assets, then any debts and insurance gaps. Sound okay, Mary?

Advisor: Hi William, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Thanks for making the time today.
Client 1: Hi Alex. Yeah, thanks for seeing us.
Client 2: Hi. Sorry, we were a couple minutes—traffic.

Advisor: Great. And Mary, income on your side—what do you consider your gross annual income right now? Even a ballpark is fine.
Client 2: Um, I think it’s like fifteen thousand a year? Maybe a little more.
Advisor: Okay, about $15,000-ish. When you say “gross,” you mean before taxes, right?
Client 2: Wait—gross is after taxes, isn’t it?
Client 1: No—gross is before taxes, Mary.

Advisor: Okay. And—small thing—the weather's been kind of all over the place.
Client 2: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Alright—going back to what you said earlier about keeping it practical, I just want to tighten up the “people” details for the plan. Mary, can I confirm your role in the household—are you William’s spouse/partner?
Client 2: Yes, I’m the spouse—partner, yeah.
Client 1: Spouse/partner, yep.

Client 2: Yeah, that’s fine.
Advisor: Mary, last piece—your date of birth. You can just say month, day, year.
Client 2: It’s November 19th, 1973.
Client 1: Yeah, 11/19/73.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And just to be clear, Mary, that’s 1973-11-19.

Advisor: Got it—finance as the primary occupation group for you, William.
Advisor: Mary, same question for you. Are you currently working at all, or would you say you’re not working right now?
Client 2: I’m not working right now—inactive, I guess.
Advisor: Okay, inactive. And when you say not working—just to be clear, no part-time job or side gig at the moment?
Client 2: No, nothing right now.

Advisor: Alright—thanks. Going back to that income point from a minute ago, I want to make sure I’m not mixing “rental income” with your personal income, William.
Advisor: William, do you personally have any gross annual income besides the rental—like wages, consulting, anything like that?
Client 1: No wages. If you’re asking what shows up as my gross income on our side, it’s about $35,350 for me.
Client 2: Wait, I thought you said the rental was fifty thousand—so isn’t yours fifty thousand?
Client 1: No—Mary, that’s the property. My personal gross is about $35,350.

Client 1: Yes, $35,350.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Let me jot that down.

Client 1: Finance. I’m finance.
Client 2: I mean—he did some operations stuff too, but yeah, finance is the main one.

Client 2: That one makes me nervous, honestly.
